name: FROST Threshold Signatures (EACB)
overview: EACB implements FROST (Flexible Round-Optimized Schnorr Threshold) signatures for N-of-M human-in-the-loop authorization of high-consequence autonomous operations. When an AI agent or automated process attempts a governed action (reclassification, deletion, cross-jurisdiction transfer), EACB requires cryptographic approval from a threshold of authorized human signers before the operation proceeds.

howItWorks.title: Operation Classification
howItWorks.description: When a governed operation is requested, the CBAC layer classifies it by consequence level. Operations above the configured threshold (e.g., bulk reclassification, data deletion) trigger EACB approval flow.

howItWorks.title: Signing Request Distribution
howItWorks.description: EACB distributes a signing request to all M authorized signers. Each request includes the operation description, affected entities, requestor identity, and a cryptographic commitment to the operation parameters.

howItWorks.title: Threshold Collection
howItWorks.description: Signers produce partial FROST signatures using their key shares. Once N-of-M partial signatures are collected, they are combined into a single valid Schnorr signature. No single signer can approve alone.

howItWorks.title: Execution Gate
howItWorks.description: The combined signature is verified against the group public key. If valid, the operation proceeds. The signature and all partial contributions are recorded in the provenance chain for audit.

comparisons.narrative: Multi-party authorization in traditional systems uses approval workflows (email chains, Slack approvals) that lack cryptographic non-repudiation. EACB provides mathematically provable N-of-M authorization where no single party (including the system operator) can forge approval. This is critical for AI governance where autonomous agents need human-gated authorization for high-consequence actions.

competitors.approach: Multi-factor auth + approval workflows via Step Functions
competitors.limitation: No threshold cryptography; approvals are advisory, not cryptographically binding

competitors.approach: Shamir's Secret Sharing for unseal keys
competitors.limitation: Unseal only, not per-operation; reconstructed key is a single point of compromise
Blockchain latency (seconds to minutes); gas costs; not suitable for database operations
